questions.stem: A quadratic form in $n$ variables is an expression that can be written as
options: $x^T A x$ for a symmetric matrix $A$
$A x = b$ for a square matrix $A$
questions.explanation: A quadratic form is $Q(x) = x^T A x$, a homogeneous degree-two polynomial in the entries of $x$, and $A$ may be taken symmetric without loss of generality. The expression $c^T x$ is linear, $Ax = b$ is a linear system, and $\nabla f = 0$ is an optimality condition, so only $x^T A x$ is a quadratic form.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: When we associate a symmetric matrix $A$ with a quadratic form, why is $A$ taken to be symmetric?
options: Because only symmetric matrices are invertible
Because any quadratic form can be represented by the symmetric matrix $\tfrac{1}{2}(A + A^T)$ without changing its value
Because symmetric matrices have determinant one
Because symmetry makes the form linear
questions.explanation: For any matrix $A$, the value $x^T A x$ equals $x^T \tfrac{1}{2}(A + A^T) x$, and $\tfrac{1}{2}(A + A^T)$ is symmetric, so we lose nothing by assuming symmetry. Symmetry does not force invertibility or unit determinant, and it certainly does not make the form linear, so the representation reason is correct.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: A symmetric matrix $A$ is positive definite if
questions.explanation: Positive definiteness means the form is strictly positive for every nonzero vector, that is $x^T A x > 0$ for all $x \ne 0$. Allowing equality gives only positive semidefinite, the strict negative case is negative definite, and a positive determinant alone is not sufficient, so the strict positivity is the definition.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: By the leading-principal-minor (Sylvester) test, a symmetric matrix is positive definite when
options: all leading principal minors are positive
the leading principal minors alternate in sign starting negative
questions.explanation: Sylvester's criterion says a symmetric matrix is positive definite exactly when every leading principal minor $D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n$ is strictly positive. Alternating signs starting negative is the test for negative definiteness, while the trace and a single determinant are not enough on their own, so all-positive minors is the correct rule.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: By the eigenvalue test, a symmetric matrix is negative definite if and only if
options: at least one eigenvalue is zero
questions.explanation: Since a symmetric matrix is orthogonally diagonalizable, the form $x^T A x$ is a weighted sum of squares with the eigenvalues as weights, so it is negative for all nonzero $x$ exactly when every eigenvalue is negative. All-positive eigenvalues give positive definite, mixed signs give indefinite, and a zero eigenvalue rules out definiteness, so all-negative is correct.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: For $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$, the leading principal minors are $D_1 = 2$ and $D_2 = 6$. The matrix is
questions.explanation: Here $D_1 = 2 > 0$ and $D_2 = \det(A) = 6 > 0$, so both leading principal minors are positive. By Sylvester's criterion the matrix is positive definite, which the eigenvalues $2$ and $3$ confirm since both are positive.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Classify $A = \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -5 \end{pmatrix}$ using its leading principal minors.
questions.explanation: The minors are $D_1 = -2 < 0$ and $D_2 = (-2)(-5) = 10 > 0$, so the signs alternate starting negative, which is the criterion for negative definiteness. The eigenvalues $-2$ and $-5$ are both negative, confirming the matrix is negative definite.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Classify $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: The determinant is $D_2 = (1)(1) - (2)(2) = -3 < 0$, and a negative second minor forces indefiniteness. The eigenvalues are $-1$ and $3$, of opposite sign, so the form takes both positive and negative values and the matrix is indefinite.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: The eigenvalues of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ are $1$ and $3$. Therefore $A$ is
questions.explanation: Both eigenvalues $1$ and $3$ are strictly positive, so by the eigenvalue test the matrix is positive definite. Equivalently the minors $D_1 = 2$ and $D_2 = 3$ are positive, agreeing with the classification.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Classify $A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, whose determinant is $0$.
questions.explanation: Here $D_1 = 4 > 0$ but $D_2 = (4)(1) - (2)(2) = 0$, so the matrix is not positive definite yet is not indefinite. The eigenvalues are $0$ and $5$, both nonnegative, so the form is always $\ge 0$ and the matrix is positive semidefinite.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: For $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, compute $D_2 = \det(A)$ and classify the matrix.
questions.explanation: The determinant is $D_2 = (2)(2) - (3)(3) = 4 - 9 = -5$, which is negative. A negative second leading minor makes the matrix indefinite, consistent with eigenvalues $-1$ and $5$ of opposite sign.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Classify $A = \begin{pmatrix} -3 & 1 \\ 1 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$ using minors.
questions.explanation: The minors are $D_1 = -3 < 0$ and $D_2 = (-3)(-3) - (1)(1) = 9 - 1 = 8 > 0$, alternating in sign starting negative. This is the negative-definite pattern, and the eigenvalues $-2$ and $-4$ are both negative, confirming it.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: The matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$ gives the quadratic form $Q(x, y) = x^2 - y^2$. This form is
questions.explanation: At $(1, 0)$ the form equals $1 > 0$ while at $(0, 1)$ it equals $-1 < 0$, so it takes both signs and is indefinite. The eigenvalues are $1$ and $-1$, of opposite sign, which is exactly the indefinite case.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: A symmetric matrix is positive semidefinite if and only if its eigenvalues are
questions.explanation: Positive semidefinite means $x^T A x \ge 0$ for all $x$, which happens exactly when every eigenvalue is $\ge 0$, allowing zeros. Requiring strictly positive eigenvalues gives positive definite, and the other options describe different sign patterns, so all-nonnegative is correct.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: For the $3 \times 3$ matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$, the leading principal minors are $D_1 = 1$, $D_2 = 2$, $D_3 = 6$. The matrix is
questions.explanation: All three leading principal minors $1$, $2$, $6$ are positive, so Sylvester's criterion gives positive definiteness. The eigenvalues are the diagonal entries $1$, $2$, $3$, all positive, confirming the result.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: For $A = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$, the leading principal minors are $D_1 = -1$, $D_2 = 2$, $D_3 = -6$. The matrix is
questions.explanation: The minors are $-1, 2, -6$, alternating in sign and starting negative, which is the negative-definite pattern for a $3 \times 3$ matrix. The eigenvalues $-1, -2, -3$ are all negative, confirming negative definiteness.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Classify $A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: The eigenvalues are the diagonal entries $3$, $-1$, $2$, which include both positive and negative values. Mixed-sign eigenvalues mean the form takes both signs, so the matrix is indefinite; indeed $D_2 = (3)(-1) = -3 < 0$ already rules out definiteness.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: The tridiagonal matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ has leading principal minors $D_1 = 2$, $D_2 = 3$, $D_3 = 4$. Classify it.
questions.explanation: All three minors $2$, $3$, $4$ are positive, so the matrix is positive definite by Sylvester's criterion. Its eigenvalues are $2 - \sqrt{2}$, $2$, and $2 + \sqrt{2}$, all positive, which agrees.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: If a symmetric matrix has one positive and one negative eigenvalue, then it is
questions.explanation: Eigenvalues of opposite sign mean the quadratic form is positive in one eigen-direction and negative in another, so it takes both signs. By definition this is an indefinite matrix, regardless of the magnitudes involved.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: For $A = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$, the leading principal minors are $D_1 = 5$ and $D_2 = 14$. The matrix is
questions.explanation: Both minors $D_1 = 5$ and $D_2 = (5)(3) - (1)(1) = 14$ are positive, so the matrix is positive definite. The eigenvalues, near $2.59$ and $5.41$, are both positive, confirming the conclusion.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Which statement about a negative-definite matrix $A$ is true?
questions.explanation: If $A$ is negative definite then $x^T A x < 0$ for all $x \ne 0$, so $x^T(-A)x > 0$ and $-A$ is positive definite. A negative-definite matrix has no zero eigenvalue and is not positive semidefinite, and its determinant sign depends on the dimension, so only the $-A$ statement holds.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: The quadratic form $Q(x, y) = 2x^2 + 4xy + 3y^2$ corresponds to which symmetric matrix?
questions.explanation: Writing $Q = x^T A x$ with symmetric $A$, the diagonal entries are the coefficients of $x^2$ and $y^2$, namely $2$ and $3$, while the off-diagonal entry is half the $xy$ coefficient, that is $\tfrac{1}{2}(4) = 2$. So $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$, where putting the full $4$ off the diagonal would double-count the cross term.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Evaluate the quadratic form $Q(x) = x^T A x$ with $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ at $x = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explanation: Compute $Ax = \begin{pmatrix} 2 + 2 \\ 1 + 6 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix}$, then $x^T(Ax) = (1)(4) + (2)(7) = 4 + 14 = 18$. Equivalently $2(1)^2 + 2(1)(2) + 3(2)^2 = 2 + 4 + 12 = 18$, so the value is $18$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Classify $A = \begin{pmatrix} 6 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ from its minors $D_1 = 6$, $D_2 = 14$.
questions.explanation: Since $D_1 = 6 > 0$ and $D_2 = (6)(3) - (2)(2) = 18 - 4 = 14 > 0$, both leading minors are positive and the matrix is positive definite. The eigenvalues are $2$ and $7$, both positive, confirming this.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: The matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, giving $Q(x, y) = (x + y)^2$, is
questions.explanation: The form $(x + y)^2$ is never negative but equals zero along the line $x = -y$, for instance at $(1, -1)$, so it is not strictly positive. With eigenvalues $0$ and $2$ and minors $D_1 = 1$, $D_2 = 0$, the matrix is positive semidefinite but not positive definite.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Classify $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ using leading principal minors and eigenvalues.
questions.solution: The first leading principal minor is $D_1 = 2$ and the second is $D_2 = \det(A) =$ {{1}}. The eigenvalues, from $\lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 3 = 0$, are $\lambda =$ {{2}} and $\lambda = 3$. Since both minors are positive and both eigenvalues are positive, the matrix is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Here $D_2 = (2)(2) - (1)(1) = 3$, and the characteristic equation gives $\lambda = 1$ and $\lambda = 3$. Positive minors and positive eigenvalues both certify positive definiteness.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Classify $A = \begin{pmatrix} -3 & 1 \\ 1 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.solution: The minors are $D_1 = -3$ and $D_2 =$ {{1}}. The eigenvalues are $\lambda = -2$ and $\lambda =$ {{2}}. Because the minors alternate in sign starting negative and both eigenvalues are negative, the matrix is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The determinant is $D_2 = (-3)(-3) - (1)(1) = 8$, and the eigenvalues solve $\lambda^2 + 6\lambda + 8 = 0$, giving $-2$ and $-4$. The alternating minors and all-negative eigenvalues mark the matrix as negative definite.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Classify $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.solution: The second leading principal minor is $D_2 = \det(A) =$ {{1}}. The eigenvalues are $\lambda = -1$ and $\lambda =$ {{2}}. A negative determinant and eigenvalues of opposite sign show the matrix is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The determinant is $D_2 = 4 - 9 = -5$, and the eigenvalues from $\lambda^2 - 4\lambda - 5 = 0$ are $-1$ and $5$. Opposite-sign eigenvalues, and equivalently a negative $D_2$, make the matrix indefinite.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Classify $A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, paying attention to the boundary case.
questions.solution: The minors are $D_1 = 4$ and $D_2 =$ {{1}}. The eigenvalues are $\lambda = 0$ and $\lambda =$ {{2}}. Because one eigenvalue is zero while the rest are nonnegative, the matrix is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Here $D_2 = (4)(1) - (2)(2) = 0$, and the eigenvalues solve $\lambda^2 - 5\lambda = 0$, giving $0$ and $5$. A zero eigenvalue with the others nonnegative means the matrix is positive semidefinite but not definite.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Use the Sylvester (leading-principal-minor) test on the $3 \times 3$ matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.solution: The leading principal minors are $D_1 = 2$, $D_2 =$ {{1}}, and $D_3 = \det(A) =$ {{2}}. Since all three are positive, the matrix is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The top-left $1 \times 1$ minor is $2$, the $2 \times 2$ minor is $(2)(2) - (1)(1) = 3$, and the full determinant expands to $4$. All positive minors give positive definiteness by Sylvester's criterion.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)

questions.stem: Evaluate and interpret the quadratic form for $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ at $x = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.solution: Expanding, $Q(x, y) = 2x^2 + 2xy + 3y^2$, so at $(1, 2)$ the value is $Q =$ {{1}}. Because the minors $D_1 = 2$ and $D_2 = 5$ are both positive, this form is {{2}}, so $Q$ is positive at every nonzero point.
questions.explanation: At $(1, 2)$ the form is $2(1) + 2(2) + 3(4) = 2 + 4 + 12 = 18$. With $D_1 = 2 > 0$ and $D_2 = (2)(3) - (1)(1) = 5 > 0$, the matrix is positive definite, so the form stays positive away from the origin.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Optimization I course (MATH 465)
